channel.title: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework
channel.description: SWARM: System-Wide Assessment of Risk in Multi-agent systems - A Distributional AGI Safety framework

image.title: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: When the Crowd Amplifies the Worst — and When the Metric Doesn't Hold
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: MiroShark's agents never down-vote, so we derived acceptance from amplification. Single runs showed a concentrated libel cascade more adversely selected than diffuse red-teaming — but a preregistered fixed-regime multi-seed follow-up did NOT replicate that libel<redteam<0 ordering: it is regime-fragile, not a property. The durable result is the non-circular amplification metric and a cautionary tale about single-seed LLM-judged safety metrics.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Halving the Entry Fee Breaks Screening Completely. Here's the Phase Transition.
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: Halving contract signing costs flips infiltration from 0% to 100%. We found the phase transition using agent-lens trajectory capture across three governance regimes.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: SimWorld's Delivery Agents Look Profitable. They're Also Adversely Selected.
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: We ran a NeurIPS 2025 delivery economy through SWARM's safety metrics. Profit says everything is fine. Adverse selection says 17% of high-value orders go to low-reputation agents.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Why Agent Infrastructure Could Be a $10B Category
item.description: Why self-evolving agent infrastructure could become a category-defining enterprise platform — and the research stack needed to validate it.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: The Shape of the Capability–Safety Frontier
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: 1,400 benchmark runs trace the capability-safety Pareto frontier across four task types. Governance costs compound nonlinearly for long-horizon tasks. Screening protocols push the frontier outward by doing information work.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Transparency Stabilizes Escalation — But Only With Safety Training
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: A 120-run sweep across 4 intelligence asymmetry conditions and 3 persona pairings reveals that transparency is decisive for safety-trained models but irrelevant for...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Does Model Size Matter for Safety?
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: 120 LLM mirror-match runs across 6 models (8B to 405B) reveal an inverse relationship: small models are more deceptive but escalate less; large models are...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Deontological Framing Reduces Deception by 95%
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: 180 LLM runs test 6 prompt framings to reduce signal-action divergence. Deontological framing nearly eliminates deception (div: 1.151 to 0.057), but...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Three Turns of Forced Cooperation Eliminate Escalation Spirals
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: 210 LLM runs reveal a universal phase transition: 3 turns of unconditional cooperation is the critical threshold that eliminates nuclear escalation...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Deception Is Structural, Not a Sampling Artifact
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: A 120-run temperature sweep across 3 escalation scenarios finds that signal-action divergence persists at T=0.0 — and in one scenario, deterministic...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: No Governance Prevents Nuclear Exchange When a Hawk Is Present
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: A 240-run parameter sweep across 5 governance levers, 4 persona pairings, and 6 governance regimes reveals that parametric governance can only prevent...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: LLMs Are More Deceptive Than Scripted Agents
item.author: SWARM Team
item.description: A 100-run comparison across 5 crisis scenarios finds that LLM agents exhibit 2x higher signal-action divergence than scripted baselines, while governance...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Six Frontier Models Played a Bluffing Game
item.description: ClashAI's Coup arena is an accidental alignment testbed --- and the first results suggest RLHF'd models would rather lose than lie.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Hodoscope Trajectory Analysis
item.description: Our self-optimizer scenario has a known punchline: three opportunistic agents game the system for 20 epochs, every hard metric passes, and toxicity hovers...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Tierra: Can Governance Improve on Natural Selection?
item.description: A red-team evaluation of our Tierra artificial life scenario gave it a split grade: A- for evolutionary resilience (adversarial genomes go extinct...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Skill Activation Is the Bottleneck
item.description: Your agent skills work 96% of the time — when they fire. The problem is getting them to fire.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: When Replicator Dynamics Meet Soft Labels
item.description: Plugging evolutionary game theory into the swarm pipeline reveals where classical predictions break down --- and where they don't
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: We Let a Coding Agent Improve Itself 5 Times. Every Fix Made It Harder to Govern.
item.description: Recursive self-improvement AI risks: we let a coding agent improve itself 5 times. Each iteration made governance harder. Measurable distributional safety implications for recursive AI systems.
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: Live LLM Scoring in Social Dilemma Norms: Synthetic vs Ollama vs OpenRouter
item.description: We wired live LLM evaluation into the social dilemma norms study and compared three scoring modes: synthetic (pre-computed corpus), local Ollama (llama3.2...
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework

item.title: The Entry Fee That Keeps Adversaries Out of the Fair Division Pool
item.description: A parameter sweep reveals a sharp screening threshold: below fee=6.0 every agent joins; above it, adversarials self-select out
item.source: SWARM — Open-Source Multi-Agent AI Safety Framework
